claim: Two-fold degeneracy is uniformly explained by bit-5 difference in GF(2)^6
Four-fold degeneracy corresponds to shared 4-bit prefix exhausting GF(2)^2
Serine is uniquely disconnected at epsilon=1 in all known genetic codes
Serine uniqueness is statistically unlikely under random codon assignment
Serine disconnection is encoding-invariant (all 24 base-to-bit mappings)
Threonine is disconnected in yeast mitochondrial code (Table 3, epsilon=2)
Leucine is disconnected in chlorophycean mitochondrial codes (translation tables 16 and 22, epsilon=2)
Alanine is disconnected in Pachysolen nuclear code (Table 26, epsilon=3)
Serine has three components in alternative yeast nuclear code (Table 12)
Codon reassignment events show directional bias in GF(2)^6 bit positions, but signal is marginal after correcting for non-independence (de-duplicated p=0.075)
Reassignment Hamming paths have characteristic length distribution
Disconnection epsilon correlates positively with evolutionary age
KRAS G12V Fano partner (His/CAC) is enriched in co-occurring mutations
Most single-codon reassignments preserve algebraic structure (feasibility > 0.5)
Serine disconnection is robust to most single-codon perturbations
